issues.title: Flutter needs a way to test real IME text input behavior
comments: @justinmc @Renzo-Olivares
Can robolectric simulate real ime behavior? Even if we do, this would require a special build the flutter test binary to include android embedding, this seems like a really big change.

I can't speak to the scope of the changes, or the options available to make this happen. Maybe the integration could be provided as a package, and maybe the Flutter engine just needs some hooks. I don't know.

What I do know is that we're getting bombarded with IME editing issues that we can't diagnose, and we're forced to ship behaviors that we can't test. Imagine that kind of restriction anywhere else in Flutter. What if it were impossible to write tests with simulated gestures, or the passage of time. Clearly there are certain things that simply must be testable within a UI toolkit, with confidence. I think the only reason that software keyboard interaction hasn't been critical within Flutter is because Flutter's history is mostly based on very primitive text field text editing. Now we're building document editors. We need this testing capability to effectively deliver Flutter solutions that edit non-trivial content. If that's a big change, then that's a big change...

isn't the current integration_test package with adb command good enough for this test? or is the ask to be able to run this as unit test? Since the ime depends on platform behavior, given the amount of platform code the test, it may as well run the real integration test instead. Some IME behaviors are also device-specific anyway.
What's a "unit test"?
I meant tests that don't involve a real device or emulator.
I'm talking about integration tests - meaning Flutter widget tests that are run with the `integration_test` package.

This has been on my mind for a long time.  We've had a lot of bugs that we're not able to fully test without something like this.  For example, bugs that depend on using a specific keyboard or language and pressing certain keys (Samsung Korean keyboard issues come to mind, or maybe this Japanese one https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/125765).

I think we could use adb to press a coordinate location on the screen in order to press specific keys on a specific keyboard.  I don't know if that approach would be worthwhile considering that it's pretty vulnerable to flakes, though.

Have you run any Espresso tests to see where Espresso hooks into the OS?
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/52694608/how-to-presskey-editor-action-on-espresso

Espresso might bypass the IME like Flutter does, but maybe not. Definitely worth checking out.

Beyond Espresso - has anyone talked to the Android team about foundational Android instrumentation? I believe that Espresso and Robolectric and things like this are all abstractions built on the core (real) instrumentation system. Maybe there are hooks at the root level that could do this.

PS - This shouldn't only be for Android. This is critical on iOS, too.

PPS - If there's truly no tooling provided by the mobile OSes to do this, on thing worth considering is taking a screenshot of the screen, running OCR that's trained against mobile keyboards, and then using the OCR to find tappable regions. I imagine that would greatly reduce flakes, and also increase the applicability of tests across many more devices with different sizes and aspect ratios.

Good ideas.  I haven't looked into Espresso.

I asked around [on Discord](https://discord.com/channels/608014603317936148/846507907876257822/1136393306956382330) and got an [old example](https://qxf2.com/blog/android-appium-press-enter-soft-keyboard/) of someone else using fixed coordinates to tap the submit key.  So at least we're not the only ones thinking of doing that.
This is another issue where text input support would help us: https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/issues/1446

issues.title: Allow Autocomplete and RawAutocomplete to distinguish onFocus and onTap events
issues.body:
### Use case

Currently both `Autocomplete` and `RawAutocomplete` widgets trigger the `optionsBuilder` callback when widget becomes focused and then a dropdown with the list of options is immediately being shown after that.

However the widget could get focus in a several different ways:

* tap on the widget (both on mobile or web/desktop)
* using keyboard and tabbing through the form fields (this is especially relevant on web/desktop)

For the latter, it is not always desirable to have options immediately shown. Especially with an async `optionsBuilder` that may make a network call.

### Proposal

It would be great to have an option in both `Autocomplete` and `RawAutocomplete` widgets to control onFocus vs onTap behaviour.

Additionally it would make sense to have `AutocompleteNextOptionIntent` and `AutocompletePreviousOptionIntent` to trigger the `optionsBuilder`, so we could have custom UI (e.g. a dropdown chevron) or custom action shortcuts (e.g. Ctrl-Down as in common Windows shortcut for dropdowns fields) to open the list of options.
comments:
AutoComplete should have the FocusNode property settable.
Currently, it changes the FocusNode of TextField with each screen update, which compromises control.
If you set the FocusNode in the TextField, the component stops working, so the suggestion is to create the property in AutoComplete.

Hi @ekuleshov , #146995 adds the ability to set the `focusNode` and `textEditingController`.   

I wanted to discuss your proposal for `AutocompleteNextOptionIntent` and `AutocompletePreviousOptionIntent` to trigger the `optionsBuilder`..  This should be possible with #146995 because you will be able to trigger the `optionsBuilder` at any time by triggering a `notifyListeners()` on the `textEditingController`  (because `RawAutocomplete` places a listener on the `textEditingController` and triggers the options builder sequence whenever it is called..)
Additionally, you should be able to control the options popping up by just returning no options in the `optionsBuilder` callback whenever you do not want the options to display (and vice versa).  (In this way you could use a `Shortcuts` widget to wrap your view widget and catch Ctrl-DownArrow to trigger the options opening/and closing).

Does that make sense ?
I would like to keep #146995 as simple as possible and still also address this issue as well.

@timmaffett I don't think drop down activation should be tied to either `focusNode` or `textEditingController`

* the focusNode is being hit when you simply navigate through element. On mobile devices without keyboard that is not be distinguishable from a tap event on a widget. But keyboard navigation, especially on desktop and web, changes things significantly. The autocomplete widget may get focused, but it does not mean it should automatically show the dropdown
* the textEditingController has the field text, so in order to activate it we basically have to change its state, e.g. delete the text and rely on the option builder to create options based on that text. E.g. in order to see all options you basically have to erase text (not desirable option). Plus dropdown is not being triggered when user previously hid it, e.g. by tapping Esc button with keyboard navigation.

With the above I am not sure how it would be possible to implement a chevron-like button in the autocomplete to trigger the dropdown visibility without changing text field content.

Here is a dartpad example of a proof of concept:

https://dartpad.dev/?id=f679a62ef5aba7d31e39569cf98e4432

This is the text field in app header RawAutocomplete example with two things added:
1) chevron to open/close the options dropdown.
2) clicking on the text fields automatically opens the dropdown.

> Here is a dartpad example of a proof of concept:
> 
> https://dartpad.dev/?id=f679a62ef5aba7d31e39569cf98e4432
> 
> This is the text field in app header RawAutocomplete example with two things added:
> 
> 1. chevron to open/close the options dropdown.
> 2. clicking on the text fields automatically opens the dropdown.

Something off with that example. It does print something about focus changes, but I was unable to see the keyboard-based dropdown interaction I described above (esc key doesn't work, and the dropdown is not getting focus upon activation).

Though generally I see several issues with this approach:

* it is kind of backwards - instead of having a definitive action/intent to show dropdown you have to fiddle with focus and text controller to show the dropdown as a side effect of the option rebuild
* it is hard to tell in the option builder if rebuild was called due to some arbitrary focus change or if it was triggered by user
* you have to create another widget wrapper and fiddle with state management which you generally don't control. I.e. you have to make assumptions how focus and text controller being updated, and they are updated by the widget you are wrapping in
* it is based on non-public API

@ekuleshov  fair enough.  I was trying to illustrate that the addition of `focusNode` and `textEditingController` allowed more possibilities.  What you are trying to accomplish requires rewrites of `RawAutocomplete` (and `Autocomplete`) at a much bigger level - adding intents and shortcuts for example to handle the keyboard interaction.

I will concede that PR #146995 does not go far enough accomplish what you desire, and i definitely do not want to make it any more complex to do so.
@victorsanni This is related to your work on controlling when the options view shows.

issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce

1. Have a Textformfield
2. Build web app
3. Attempt to insert a newline in any web browser

**Expected results:** 

A new line

**Actual results:**

Not a new line

```dart
TextFormField(
    maxLines: 4,
    minLines: 3,
    controller: editingControllers['addr'],
    onChanged: (String val) => _changed(),
    textInputAction: TextInputAction.newline,
    keyboardType: TextInputType.streetAddress,
    decoration: InputDecoration(
        alignLabelWithHint: true,
        labelText: 'Address',
        hintMaxLines: 3,
        hintText:
            '124. Conch street\n00802 Bikini Bottom\nU.S. Virgin Islands'),
),
```
I have found similar issues on github, all of which were left unanswered and automatically closed by bots.

issues.title: Accessibility: TextField value is blank before gain focus
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce 1. Create a TextField and sets an initial value using a TextEditingController 2. Using a screen reader tool (NVDA, VoiceOver, ..) content will be read as empty until it gains focus 3. If a TextField has already gained focus at least one times, the content will be read correctly ### Expected results Using a screen reader tool, i should be able to read the content inside the TextField ### Actual results TextField's content is showed but, with a screen reader, it will be read as empty. After the TextField gains focus (both navigate using TAB or clicking on it) the content will be read correctly. ### Code sample
Code sample ```dart void main() { final controller = TextEditingController(text: 'Initial content'); WidgetsFlutterBinding.ensureInitialized(); SemanticsBinding.instance.ensureSemantics(); runApp( MaterialApp( builder: (context, child) => Scaffold( body: TextField( controller: controller, ), ), ), ); } ```
### Screenshots or Video ### Logs _No response_ ### Flutter Doctor output

comments:
Thanks for the report. I can reproduce this on macOS app with VoiceOver reader. 

- When the field is unfocused, `content is empty` is read.
- When I focus on the field, the text value is read as `Initial content` (expected). But if I press Control-Option-Shift-Down Arrow keys to interact with the field, `content is empty` is read.

issues.title: [web] Support scrolling inside the text field
issues.body:
There are 2 situations where the text field can scroll:
1. Very long text in a single-line text field (horizontal scrolling).
2. Many lines of text in a multi-line text field (vertical scrolling).

Currently, the scrolling works fine but the issue is that Flutter's field and the browser's field get out of sync, and they are scrolled to different positions.
comments: Closing/Merging with the duplicate: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/40712
For horizontal scrolling one thing to do is to put <input> tag inside the glass pane.

I can't continue my flutter web project because of this issue. It has been open for nearly a year. Is there any hope of a solution soon? I'm wondering about moving back to building an Ionic PWA, but that would be a backward step I think.
Thanks for your efforts,
Andrew

issues.title: Text selection vertical extent should be defined per line (not per glyph)
issues.body:
As [reported by @_mono on Twitter]( https://twitter.com/_mono/status/1023733699614060545), the Flutter text selection highlight rect should be computed at the line level as opposed to per glyph. To see this effect, paste the sequence `(　´･‿･｀)` into a Flutter text field and select it.

Example images below:

Flutter selection:
![flutter](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/351029/45705368-424c8180-bb2e-11e8-9502-3fa404afc066.png)

iOS OEM selection:
![ios](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/351029/45705373-45e00880-bb2e-11e8-930a-156f02e88a15.png)

Android OEM selection:
![android](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/351029/45706301-bdaf3280-bb30-11e8-9ee6-07384c82395c.png)
comments: /cc @jason-simmons @garymm @xster

Based on the platform-specific behaviour, it looks like a bug to me. Both Android and iOS appear to use the line height. 

It seems potentially useful to expose the glyph-specific metrics (e.g. if you were interested in computing text metrics of a glyph independent from its context) but in terms of selection, I'd assume we should be using line-height.
Looks like the linked commit(s) were intended to support text _spans_ being treated as a single block for Rich text, but is now treating each glyph as a separate block.
Did you mean to CC someone else? I have no particular expertise or interest in this bug.
Should've been @GaryQian

I think I originally implemented it so that the height is the max of each line to match native/blink behavior. I believe this is mainly so that multi-line highlights do not leave awkward gaps between lines due to differing heights (as seen below).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1887398/45896986-b08d8000-bd8a-11e8-9d90-6f63afaa8994.png)

When spans of differing heights are stacked on top of each other, they always advance enough in the y direction to accommodate the tallest span

Also, @dnfield it seems that the (　´･‿･｀) text uses characters from different sets, so they are automatically broken into different spans. it just happens that they are alternating, making it look particularly strange.

So it sounds like we're treating it as different spans because of different sets.

I think what a user would expect (and what Android/iOS do here), is that the text runs are _styled_ the same, so they should be treated as the same height.  In other words, this text run was built from a single `ParagraphBuilder.addText` with no intervening `pushStyle` or `pop` - or, could be generated using a single `TextSpan` class.

That makes sense to me, but to match native behavior (and therefore expected), I think we still should take the max of the line heights. We could also provide the glyph/span specific heights as a separate API, but not sure if that would be particularly useful.

On native text, even if you have a font size 10 on the same line as a font size 150, the bounding boxes for that line are 150 high.
https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/6335 begins to implement a new API for more advanced GetRectsForRange, which provides the boxes for text selection.
https://github.com/flutter/engine/commit/3a9c22afde676bee3c455749101757c1814153fd has landed, but still needs to be enabled on the framework side.

https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/6644 adds API for various types of enclosing boxes. `Paragraph.getBoxesForRange(...)` takes a BoxHeightStyle and a BoxWidthStyle which define how the boxes are calculated.

The default has been left as `tight` so the current behavior will continue, but for apps that desire other behavior, custom styles can be presented to the `getBoxesForRange` method.

This also allows emulating different text selection behaviors present on mac, windows, ios, android, and so on.

@cbracken 

As commented at https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/23433#issuecomment-616003053, this isn’t fixed yet.
I can’t figure out why this was closed.
Could you re-open this to track the issue appropriately?

This thread has been automatically locked since there has not been any recent activity after it was closed. If you are still experiencing a similar issue, please open a new bug, including the output of `flutter doctor -v` and a minimal reproduction of the issue.
This doesn't appear to be fixed. /cc @GaryQian I'm guessing that we're mixing a framework PR to take advantage of the new API that was added in #6644.

issues.title: Provide API in TextField to open the keyboard
issues.body:
From https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/15882#issuecomment-433563683:

I've just discovered calling `FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus(...)` is not good enough. It does not [request for the keyboard](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/9bf8502fef3f35d8d3ba692ce0383e9f55a34624/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/text_field.dart#L491). This can be an issue in the following case:

1. Tap on text field. We provide it focus. This opens the keyboard.
2. Tap Android back button. Keyboard is dismissed. Field still has focus.
3. Tap on text field again. We try to provide it focus again but this doesn't do anything. **There is no way to get the keyboard back up.** 

In order to display the keyboard, we need to call `requestKeyboard` on the `EditableTextState` but this is [not exposed to us](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/9bf8502fef3f35d8d3ba692ce0383e9f55a34624/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/text_field.dart#L441) as the global key is private.
comments:
#20706, #19552, #7247 might be related
I think I saw similar issues mentioned starting with Android 28
Adding customer blocker tag; this appears more important to fix than #15882 given related issues.

I'm trying to understand the failing focus scenario. In [this app](https://gist.github.com/HansMuller/15522d3e9adc39f1c71af3cf88e8aa26), tapping the textfield to give it the focus and then backing out of the page that contains the text field doesn't prevent the keyboard from reappearing when the text field's page is pushed again. 

How does the failing scenario differ from this test case?

https://gist.github.com/HansMuller/15522d3e9adc39f1c71af3cf88e8aa26
cc @goderbauer
Thanks for providing a simple test case. The problem appears to be that, in this situation, we're not giving up the focus when the keyboard is dismissed. Investigating...

Although it's possible to unconditionally show the keyboard upon `FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus()` (on Android), doing so turns out to a bit complicated.

Most Android apps allow the user to hide the soft keyboard with the back button and then restore it by tapping on a text field. The user might hide the keyboard and switch to a physical keyboard, or they might just need to get it out of the way for a moment, to expose something obscured by the keyboard.

It would be helpful to understand your use case better. I'm assuming that your app wants to programmatically manage focus to control which field the user can provide input to. Could that be handled by only enabling one textfield at a time?

@HansMuller my ultimate goal is to do just what you said: allow the user to restore the keyboard by tapping on the text field. However, I need to listen to tap events on the text field, and I can't do that because of #15882. The workaround for that issue was to wrap the text field with an `IgnorePointer` widget and then call `FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus(...)`, but that is causing me to face this issue.

As you know, the TextField builds a GestureDetector to handle input events, like tap, to trigger focus requests, to move the caret, adjust the selection, etc. Obscuring some of those events with competing GestureDetector is problematic. It would be possible to give TextField "me too" callbacks for the gestures it recognizes.  That said, what's the use case for handling taps in a TextField?

My intention is not to compete with the `TextField`'s gesture detector but to receive the same tap events that its gesture detector receives.

We have a UX requirement to perform some animation every time the text field is tapped, even if it already has focus (so we can't subscribe to focus node changes).
Sounds good. That's what I was referring to as a "me too" onTap callback.
The "me too" onTextField onTapp callback is https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/24536
Since the original issue is fixed, can this one be closed?

This no longer affects us. It's up to you.

I think it's still worth leaving an issue open for providing an API to open the keyboard.
If we're going to leave this issue open, can you update the description to match "provide an API to open the keyboard" (rather than requestFocus unconditionally opens the keyboard).
Any updates on this?

@HansMuller 
I think it's a good idea to add this code `SystemChannels.textInput.invokeMethod('TextInput.show');`
whenever `requestFocus` is invoked to prevent this situation:

> 1.  Tap on text field. We provide it focus. This opens the keyboard.
> 2. Tap Android back button. Keyboard is dismissed. Field still has focus.
> 3. Tap on text field again. We try to provide it focus again but this doesn't do anything. There is no way to get the keyboard back up.

I am currently experiencing this problem too.
then tried to research things but nothing solves.

then until I understand the problem.

1. Pressed The Textfield keyboard shows.
2. Redirected to another page then Keyboard closes
3. then go back to the textfield page again and then Tap again to TextField, keyboard wont reappear anymore

the problem is I think the SDK doesnt recognized that focus is not on the textfield anymore 
so just calling this wont work 

`FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus(...);`

what I did was just add another line before it

```
FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus(FocusNode()); 
FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus(...) ;
```

you have to forcefully remove the Focus first then put it back.
it solves my problem
Any updates on this?I think this is a very common request when the comment list needs to be responded to.

What's your device? Personally I have noticed keyboard being slow even for native apps on older devices almost every iOS update.
iPhone 15 pro max, but it happens on simulator too
same here, iPhone Se2
This issue is still present in version 3.29. Please investigate.

Im noticing this issue on my iphone 16 pro. latest version of flutter in debug mode and im also getting this warning.

```
Error: this application, or a library it uses, has passed an invalid numeric value (NaN, or not-a-number) to CoreGraphics API and this value is being ignored. Please fix this problem.
If you want to see the backtrace, please set CG_NUMERICS_SHOW_BACKTRACE environmental variable.
```
@danagbemava-nc could you set this to p1 since it is still present in version 3.29

Check `FocusScope.of(context).hasFocus == true`
I faced the same problem and I found that default FocusScope has focused initially.
And then I wrapped the TextField with a FocusScope fixed the problem.

@john7964 I believe you are just deferring the lag issue from the first time the page renders (due to keyboard focus default) to the first time the keyboard is focused after that. I have found that, on master channel, it is still laggy on first keyboard open.
This would be amazing if it would get fixed. It is a shame that it has been happening for a long time now.

For anyone else encountering this issue, please add a thumbs up to the issue instead. Kindly read [https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/master/docs/contributing/issue_hygiene/README.md#comments](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/master/docs/contributing/issue_hygiene/README.md#comments) and https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/master/docs/contributing/issue_hygiene/README.md#thumbs-up-reactions

Thank you

I think I found a possible root cause for the issue. In the 2023 WWDC, Apple reworked the keyboard architecture. At the end of the explanation video, they mention that the new architecture might cause issues if the main thread is busy during the keyboard opening process — [see this point in the video.](https://youtu.be/49VhMsZoa6E?t=805)

This could explain why people are noticing the issue more often when using bottom sheets with autofocus set on a TextField. In my case, I observed that when a bottom sheet contained an autofocus TextField, the entire root Scaffold would re-render as the keyboard opened — even when resizeToAvoidBottomInset was set to false.

After completely removing the Scaffold, I now have a smooth experience. By also minimizing main thread activity during keyboard open, I’ve been able to achieve a noticeably smoother transition.
Any updates?

issues.title: Disable paste action in TextField when using three-finger gestures on iOS
issues.body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [X] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues)
- [X] I have read the [guide to filing a bug](https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports)

### Use case

Raising again the issue discussed in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/132191.
There is a way of preventing Copy/Paste by configuring (f.e.):

 ```
TextField(
       enableInteractiveSelection: false,
       contextMenuBuilder: (_, __) => const SizedBox.shrink(),
        // Other properties...
)
```

However, it is still possible to paste in the TextField using keyboard shortcuts or three-finger gestures on iOS.


### Proposal

As proposed by the issue: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/132191
A parameter that will only allow keyboard input in the TextField and block paste action.

issues.title: Keyboard Hints Disappear and Reappear When Toggling Password Visibility in Flutter TextField
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

When toggling the visibility of a password field in a Flutter app, the keyboard hints (e.g., autofill suggestions, password manager hints) disappear and reappear. This issue occurs only on iOS 18 on emulator and on physical device. 

Create a TextField with obscureText set to true for password input.

Add an IconButton to toggle obscureText between true and false.

Run the app on an iOS 18 device or simulator.

Focus on the password field to bring up the keyboard.

Tap the visibility icon to toggle password visibility.

Observe the keyboard hints (e.g., autofill suggestions) disappearing and reappearing. 


### Expected results

The keyboard hints (e.g., autofill suggestions, password manager hints) should remain stable and not disappear when toggling password visibility.

### Actual results

The keyboard hints disappear and reappear when toggling password visibility, causing a flickering effect and disrupting the user experience.

### Code sample

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(
        appBar: AppBar(title: Text('Password Field Bug')),
        body: Padding(
          padding: const EdgeInsets.all(16.0),
          child: PasswordField(),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

class PasswordField extends StatefulWidget {
  @override
  _PasswordFieldState createState() => _PasswordFieldState();
}

class _PasswordFieldState extends State<PasswordField> {
  bool _isObscured = true;

  void _toggleVisibility() {
    setState(() {
      _isObscured = !_isObscured;
    });
  }

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return TextField(
      obscureText: _isObscured,
      decoration: InputDecoration(
        hintText: "Enter your password",
        border: OutlineInputBorder(),
        suffixIcon: IconButton(
          icon: Icon(_isObscured ? Icons.visibility_off : Icons.visibility),
          onPressed: _toggleVisibility,
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

issues.title: Active subrange within composing range should be visually highlighted in Flutter
issues.body: ## Description When inputting text in languages that use multi-step input (composing mode), there are two ranges involved: 1. The overall composing range 2. The active subrange within the composing range This is common when inputting text using [ateji](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ateji) in Japanese, but occurs in general with longer composing ranges, person, and place names. To support this, we'll need some visual highlighting of the text to make clear which range is the active subrange, as well as changes to the text input protocol so that the engine can specify this range to the framework. It seems unlikely to me that users will need to programmatically control the range via TextEditingController, so ideally the API surface should be as minimal as possible. ## Repro steps and expected behaviour For example, when inputting the name 真惟佳 (Maika), one would go through the following steps: 1. Type the phonetic kana characters まいか. 2. Press shift-left arrow to restrict the active subrange within the composing range to the first character, ま. 3. Select the correct completion, then hit the right arrow to switch the active range to the remainder of the composing range. At this point the composing range reads 真いか and いか becomes the active subrange. 4. Press shift-left arrow to restrict the active range to the second character only, then select the correct completion, then hit right arrow to switch the active range to the final character. The composing region now read 真惟か and か becomes the active subrange. 5. Select the correct completion, then hit return to commit the composing region. The text 真惟佳 is now committed. ## Visual representation The composing region and active subrange are each visually highlighted to the user in some platform-specific way. On macOS 11.0, Chrome, and Android, it is highlighted using a thicker underline with a small gap between it and the thinner composing region underline. In Linux GTK apps (and I believe older versions of macOS), the subrange is highlighted with a lighter colour than the standard selection colour. macOS 11.0: iPadOS 14.0: Chrome on Linux: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/351029/96796384-8452ec00-13b4-11eb-9c3b-9ad138207b8a.png) Linux GTK apps: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/351029/96798259-5a4df980-13b5-11eb-8cff-1c33878ddec9.png)
comments: /cc @HansMuller @justinmc
I believe this is a requirement for Desktop launch in CJK locales.
CC @LongCatIsLooong
Is this something we're also missing on mobile?

> Is this something we're also missing on mobile?

Yes. This is required on devices using a physical keyboard. I've added a screenshot of the UI for iPadOS 14. I haven't tested on an iPhone using a bluetooth keyboard but that is a [supported configuration](https://support.apple.com/en-au/guide/iphone/iph2ced3177/ios).

I should have added a Windows screenshot today, but I can verify it looks effectively like Chrome.

Example video on macOS of the way users can move the active region around the comping region, and how that affects candidate menu position to give a better idea:


https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/351029/110860938-43c81200-8272-11eb-9bc9-4b073e12e274.mp4

This issue also affects Android, even without a hardware keyboard.

With the Gboard Japanese keyboard, the expected behavior is just like in the original description above, except that instead of shift-left arrow, you use the software keyboard's left and right arrows. When a composing region is active, these arrows move the end of the active region, and are restricted to within the composing region.

In a native Android app, the highlighting is:
* The whole composing region is underlined.
* The active subregion is highlighted in an aqua-green hue. (Distinct from the selection color, which is more blue.)
* The rest of the composing region, if there is any, is highlighted with a lighter version of the same color.

Here's a screenshot after step 4(a) of the repro steps above: the middle of three characters is the active region, and the last two characters together are the composing region. This is on a Pixel device running Android 13. A screenshot of a selection is at the right for comparison.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28173/224526452-3bb278fd-b88f-4572-9e6e-b1c8409623dc.png) ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28173/224527024-cf8d48de-51b3-4a9f-926e-f13c54ec1ed6.png)

(Oddly the caret is still painted at the end of the whole composing region. I checked multiple native apps and they all have this behavior. I don't know whether that's a design choice or a bug. **Edit:** Probably a bug, because it turns out that in a slightly different situation the caret gets painted in a place that surely can't be intended: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/122490#issuecomment-1465123915)

The current actual behavior in Flutter main, on Android, is that the whole composing region is underlined and there is no highlighting or anything else to indicate the active subregion:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28173/224527157-248db260-74ad-473a-b6e4-8d4647707bf3.png)



<details><summary>Current behavior in more detail</summary>

In more detail, the current actual behavior is:
* If you ignore the visual feedback in the text field itself, and just focus on the keyboard, it actually works perfectly.

  But the feedback in the text field is confusing enough that many users may never discover that.
* The whole composing region is underlined, and the caret is painted at the end of the composing region, regardless of where the active subregion is.
  * Unless, that is, you shrink the active subregion all the way to be collapsed at the start of the composing region. In that case the caret is painted there at the start.

    Curiously the odd Android-native behavior mentioned above, where the caret is always painted at the end despite the active subregion, has this same exception: if you move all the way to the start of the composing region, then the caret is painted there instead of at the end.
* There is no indication of the active subregion at all. (Unless you collapse it all the way to the start.) So when you press the left arrow to try to shrink the active range, it looks in the text field like it didn't do anything.
  * (On the other hand if you look at the keyboard's suggestions, they will have updated exactly as they should. Also if you hit delete, or type more kana, the deletion or insertion happens correctly at the end of the active subregion, even though that's not where the caret appears to be.)

</details>

A different issue that's also about styling the composing range, and highlighting vs. underlining:
* #9343
Added a minor update to the styling issue for iOS 17/macOS 14 Sonoma. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/9343
Reopening since https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/49314 only fixes the issue on macOS (where we let the IME handle the text input key events). I haven't tried to reproduce the issue on iOS.
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

This issue is marked P1 but has had no recent status updates.

The P1 label indicates high-priority issues that are at the top of the work list. This is the highest priority level a bug can have if it isn't affecting a top-tier customer or breaking the build. Bugs marked P1 are generally actively being worked on unless the assignee is dealing with a P0 bug (or another P1 bug). Issues at this level should be resolved in a matter of months and should have monthly updates on GitHub.

Please consider where this bug really falls in our current priorities, and label it or assign it accordingly. This allows people to have a clearer picture of what work is actually planned. Thanks!

issues.title: Reliable initialization of keyboard and pointer bindings
issues.body:
We should review the messaging mechanism of various bindings (keyboard and pointer) and make them reliable. The current process, where the embedding blindly sends events to the framework, might cause message loss at initialization and state instability, especially during hot restarts.

## Analysis

Take keyboard as an example. Currently, when the app is started,
1. The Flutter view creates both a keyboard manager and a dart VM.
2. The keyboard manager immediately starts listening to system key events and send messages through the embedder API, `FlutterSendKeyEvent`.
3. It takes a short while for the dart VM to boot and run through all static statements, such as assigning a listener to the framework API `onKeyData`.
4. If a key event happens between the creation of the keyboard manager and the initialization of the framework, since the listener is still null, the message is lost.
 
Since the keyboard system only sends events, not the entire state, packet losses are critical and lead to state instability.

Now, we have managed to alleviate this issue by making the keyboard system send messages through the binary messenger to make use of the message buffer (https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/29795). But the message buffer only has a depth of 1, and it's not difficult to reproduce a case that overflows the buffer (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/89748). We can increase the buffer, but that only makes the problem "harder" to occur, not impossible.

The same problem is also found in the pointer event system (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/102453#issuecomment-1117898221). We decided to temporarily alleviate this problem by relaxing the assertions.

Moreover, if the framework decides to reconstruct the keyboard manager or pointer manager, there is no way to synchronize the states.

## Proposal

There are two options to fix this issue.

1. **Send states, not deltas.** By always sending the entire keyboard/pointer state on every change, state stability is _guaranteed_, and the 1-depth message buffer can be used as intended. However these states can get really big. The user might smash the keyboard, pressing 30 keys at the same time, which becomes 60 `uint64`s per message, and the framework also needs to compare the state difference to figure out events.
2. **Initiate messaging/synchronization by framework.** The framework will send an establishment message when it first assigns a listener, and the engine will reply this message with a full state. This method complicates the API protocol, but keeps the followup messages light.

Both of the two options will possibly be a breaking change to the current embedder API.
comments: +cc @jason-simmons @gspencergoog

It'd also be great to hear @stuartmorgan 's opinion, since when we were planning for the keyboard API, we decided to put it into `embedder.h` to mark it "stable", and now we might have to break it. Ideally I'd like to move both the keyboard system and the pointer system out of embedder.h into a binary message channel. My rationales are:

1. The APIs outside of `embedder.h` should be as stable as the ones in it. Even if the API had been written in message channels instead, any changes to them would still cause a breakage.
2. Just putting APIs in `embedder.h` does not guarantee the stability either, since `embedder.h` only defines the messages from the embedding to the framework, not the other way around.
3. The keyboard system, after https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/29795, is essentially using a binary message channel, but it has to re-implement the binary encoder, because `embedder.cc` can not use the encoders.

> We can increase the buffer, but that only makes the problem "harder" to occur, not impossible.

Keyboard messages are small; is there a reason these events specifically can't use an unbounded buffer? How much memory are we realistically talking about here?

> Both of the two options will possibly be a breaking change to the current embedder API.

Why would they be breaking changes? If you want to send full state, the accumulation of that state from deltas can be done under embedder.h from the current information. If you add a new callback, when it's not set the behavior is the current behavior (no initial state).

> It'd also be great to hear @stuartmorgan 's opinion, since when we were planning for the keyboard API, we decided to put it into `embedder.h`

Adding @chinmaygarde who wanted to do that since before I joined Flutter.

> to mark it "stable"

It wasn't just to mark it stable. It makes the API more consistent, much easier to use (compile-time, typed enforcement of data passed), and decouples the stable API from implementation details of marshalling (see below).

> 1. The APIs outside of `embedder.h` should be as stable as the ones in it.

This is true, but it's much harder to keep track of.

> 2\. `embedder.h` only defines the messages from the embedding to the framework, not the other way around.

The fact that some things haven't been converted to `embedder.h` APIs yet isn't an argument against it, it just means we should convert those too 🙂 Registering callback methods is already a common pattern in `embedder.h`.

> 3\. but it has to re-implement the binary encoder, because `embedder.cc` can not use the encoders.

The alternative is:
- Every single embedder must implement the encoder we use. For instance, the Windows embedder implemented and is shipping a JSON encoder for the *sole* purpose of dealing with a few system channels that use an encoding we don't even want to be using any more (but can't easily change because it's a de-facto part of the API surface)
- We can never change the serialization details. We can't change codecs, as mentioned, but also can't change the exact format. Consider the pointer serialization for example: it's very space-efficient, but also extremely fiddly. Would you force every embedder to do all those details correctly? Or would you make it a key-value structure that's less error-prone, but probably an order of magnitude larger? We shouldn't have to make tradeoffs between API usability and marshalling efficiency.

> Keyboard messages are small; is there a reason these events specifically can't use an unbounded buffer? How much memory are we realistically talking about here?

I don't think `ChannelBuffers` supports unbounded buffer at all. Also, although a key event might be small, there can be a lot: at worst case the user can hold all 108 keys at start up and we'll have to send each of them as an initializing event. (Which doesn't matter if we add a synchronizing API.)

> Why would they be breaking changes? ...

You're right. I can call it something like "synchronize state" and nothing will be broken.

> It makes the API more consistent, much easier to use...

Can you illustrate why we require ABI stability for embedder.h? Do we compile to a .dll or something? I thought we static linked everything so if, for example a field in a struct defined in `embedder.h` is changed from a int64 to a double, the engine compiler would have caught it. Everything should work fine as long as the engine and the framework are using matching versions.

> Every single embedder must implement the encoder we use...

Off topic but in the foreseeable future we are probably keeping them.

> Can you illustrate why we require ABI stability for embedder.h?

I'm not sure what you mean by "illustrate". We require it because it's our explicit, documented policy that the embedder.h API, which is public, preserves ABI.

> Do we compile to a .dll or something?

We do compile a DLL with that API surface, yes. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Custom-Flutter-Engine-Embedders

Any anyone else is welcome to as well.

> I thought we static linked everything so if, for example a field in a struct defined in `embedder.h` is changed from a int64 to a double, the engine compiler would have caught it.

This is true for our own uses of the API. It is not true in general.
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

issues.title: IME deltas on Android trigger input method restarts when they shouldn't
issues.body:
I'm integrating the relatively new IME deltas in Flutter into a document editor. I'm finding that on Android (not iOS), that it's essentially impossible to keep Android from restarting the input method due to changes to the composing region.

For example, I have a blob of text. I place the caret in the text. Then I press and hold the backspace button on the Android software keyboard. The expected behavior is that delete deltas keep getting reported until all the available text is gone. In reality, at random times, the Flutter Android embedding decides that it doesn't like the composing region and forces the input method to restart. This abruptly causes the deletion to stop.

The deletion problem is exceptionally visible when you place the caret at the very end of the text. In that case, the deletion only makes it 1 character before the input method restarts. This essentially means that if a user wants to delete a bunch of text, including the last character in the text, then that user needs to press the backspace button for every single character.

The offending code in the Android embedding is here:
https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/main/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/plugin/editing/TextInputPlugin.java#L469

To mitigate this problem, I've tried to send composing regions to the OS that match what the OS wants. Unfortunately, mismatches still show up. At this moment, I don't even care about composing regions. The OS can do whatever it wants with composing regions. But I need the input method to stop restarting itself.

You'll know that this issue is triggered when the following appears in your run output:
```
I/TextInputPlugin( 8040): Composing region changed by the framework. Restarting the input method.
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): beginBatchEdit on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): getTextBeforeCursor on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): getTextAfterCursor on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): getSelectedText on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): endBatchEdit on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): beginBatchEdit on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): endBatchEdit on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): beginBatchEdit on inactive InputConnection
W/IInputConnectionWrapper( 8040): endBatchEdit on inactive InputConnection

```

**Reproduction code:** I know the team wants a minimal reproduction, but each time that I file one of the IME delta issues it's putting me in a position where I have to copy/paste an entire `TextInputClient` implementation. I don't think that's a good use of my time. If someone on the team can demonstrate text input and deletion using IME deltas without running into this problem, I'd like to see it.

Flutter version:
```
Flutter 2.10.0 • channel stable • https://github.com/flutter/flutter.git
Framework • revision 5f105a6ca7 (2 days ago) • 2022-02-01 14:15:42 -0800
Engine • revision 776efd2034
Tools • Dart 2.16.0 • DevTools 2.9.2
```

comments: There're three cases that the `PhysicalKeyboardKey` is not correct. 1. Swith to ko/zh/ja softkeyboard and click "Backspace"/"Enter" in the physical keyboard. 2. Swith to ko/zh/ja softkeyboard, input any text in the text field, click "Backspace" in the soft keyboard. 3. Just empty the input textfield, switch to en input method, click "Backspace" in the soft keyboard.

Hi @fufesou 
I checked this on my setup (Pixel 7, Android 14 connect with bluetooth keyboard Keychron K1) or simpler (Pixel 7, Android 14 and cast on macbook screen with scrcpy), and I see the same PhysicalKeyboardKey event with all languages:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/61929792-952b-4dc3-b78b-ef7ea46922c4
</details>

This is weird. I did the same thing, except I used an emulator. Your "PhysicalKeyboardKey" seems to be wrong for all input methods. I'm sure the correct `PhysicalKeyboardKey` should be `PhysicalKeyboardKey (usbHidUsage: "0x00070028", debugName: "Enter")`. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/97596e589561b508b7b15cd6c6f04ecf5a5e15e2/packages/flutter/lib/src/services/keyboard_key.g.dart#L4044 `event.physicalKey.usbHidUsage & 0xFFFF` should be the value of the following pdf. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/97596e589561b508b7b15cd6c6f04ecf5a5e15e2/packages/flutter/lib/src/services/keyboard_key.g.dart#L3543

issues.title: Make shortcuts portable across different keyboard layouts
issues.body:
Flutter shortcuts defined with `SingleActivator` or `LogicalKeySet` are not keyboard layout agnostic. For example, the classic zoom-in shortcut `Control`+`+` only works with such keyboard layouts (e.g. Finnish) that do not require the `Shift` modifier to be pressed to access `+`. If the system has e.g. US English keyboard layout in use, the shortcut has to be defined as `Control`+`Shift`+`+` instead. A single shortcut definition does not work with both keyboard layouts.

The "logical" shortcut is `Control`+`+` and what is typically presented in GUIs. Application developers should not need to know that in some keyboard layouts, the "physical" key combination to activate the shortcut includes an "extra" `Shift` modifier.

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- Please include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Switch to Finnish keyboard layout
2. Execute `flutter run` on the code sample <!-- (see "Code sample" section below) -->
2. Try pressing `Control`+`+` and `Control`+`-`
3. Observe "zoom in" and "zoom out" in the debug output
4. Switch to US English keyboard layout
5. Try pressing `Control`+`+` and `Control`+`-`
6. Observe the debug output. "zoom in" doesn't get activated

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->

`Control`+`+` should work regardless of the keyboard layout.

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->

- `Control`+`+` works with the Finnish keyboard layout:
  ```dart
  SingleActivator(LogicalKeyboardKey.add, control: true)
  ```
vs.

- `Control`+`Shift`+`+` works with the US English keyboard layout:
  ```dart
  SingleActivator(LogicalKeyboardKey.add, control: true, shift: true)
  ```

<details>
<summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
import 'package:flutter/services.dart';

const zoomIn = SingleActivator(LogicalKeyboardKey.add, control: true);
const zoomOut = SingleActivator(LogicalKeyboardKey.minus, control: true);

void main() {
  runApp(
    MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(
        body: CallbackShortcuts(
          bindings: {
            zoomIn: () => print('zoom in'),
            zoomOut: () => print('zoom out'),
          },
          child: const Focus(autofocus: true, child: SizedBox.shrink()),
        ),
      ),
    ),
  );
}
```
</details>

<details>
  <summary>Logs</summary>

```
[✓] Flutter (Channel main, 3.7.0-3.0.pre.57, on Ubuntu 22.10 5.19.0-23-generic, locale en_DK.UTF-8)
```

comments: @gspencergoog It sounds right that Shortcuts should not require "shift" to match control-+ if the plus key doesn't require "shift".  Is this something that we could support in Shortcuts, or have you thought about this before?

You are correct: both `LogicalKeySet` and `SingleActivator` are not entirely keyboard layout agnostic.

If you want to "Ctrl-+" to be the shortcut, based on the character "+" that it produces, rather than on the key combination on the keyboards that support it, then you should use a `CharacterActivator` and not a `SingleActivator` (at this point `LogicalKeySet` is only around for legacy reasons).

A [`CharacterActivator`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/CharacterActivator-class.html) will activate if the key sequence produces the character given, so for `Ctrl-+`, you would specifcy `CharacterActivator('+', control: true)`.

issues.title: TextField inside CheckboxListTile results in flakey calls to onChanged
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Create a `CheckboxListTile` with an `onChanged` that changes its `selected` property, and its `title` being a `TextField`.
2. Click on the `TextField`. (I have only tried this on web on desktop.)

### Expected results

I would expect consistent behavior: either `onChanged` is called or it is not. Out of these options I would probably expect it not to be called: I think the user is probably trying to edit the text in the `TextField` without changing the selection/checkbox status of the `CheckboxListTile`.

### Actual results

_Sometimes_ the `onChanged` handler is called, and _sometimes_ it is not. There doesn't seem to be a clear pattern. It can happen when the `TextField` already has focus, or if it's receiving focus via the tap.

I suspect this has to do with how things are resolved in the gesture arena. If one wraps the `TextField` in a `GestureDetector`:

```dart
title: GestureDetector(
  behavior: HitTestBehavior.opaque,
  onTap: () {},
  child: const TextField(),
)
```

then the problem seems to disappear (but it's hard to be sure because of the flakeyness).

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() => runApp(const CheckboxListTileApp());

class CheckboxListTileApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const CheckboxListTileApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      theme: ThemeData(useMaterial3: true),
      home: const CheckboxListTileExample(),
    );
  }
}

class CheckboxListTileExample extends StatefulWidget {
  const CheckboxListTileExample({super.key});

  @override
  State<CheckboxListTileExample> createState() => _CheckboxListTileExampleState();
}

class _CheckboxListTileExampleState extends State<CheckboxListTileExample> {
  bool isChecked = false;
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(title: const Text('CheckboxListTile Sample')),
      body: Center(
        child: CheckboxListTile(
          value: isChecked,
          selected: isChecked,
          selectedTileColor: Colors.green[100],
          onChanged: (bool? value) {
            setState(() => isChecked = value!);
          },
          title: const TextField(),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

</details>


### Screenshots or Video

<summary>Screenshots / Video demonstration</summary>

@olof-dev I wonder if the tap that happens at 38 seconds is just outside of the field while still being inside of the CheckboxListTile? Does it reproduce more consistently if you tap at the very top?

@justinmc I think the cursor is still within the TextField for all the problematic clicks -- certainly the mouse cursor thinks so, showing up as a 'caret'. (It **is** possible to click above or below the TextField but still within the CheckboxListTile, and then `onChanged` is called with 100% consistency. The mouse cursor then shows up as a pointer: see 0:37 in the video, for example.) But yeah, the clicks at 42 s, 49 s, 1:09 are all definitely within the bounds of the TextField.

issues.title: [Proposal] Add a `text` parameter to `TextField` and `TextFormField` to update their values
comments: This approach introduces limitations for more advanced TextField use cases. If implemented, it might be provided as an optional feature for a basic scenario.

What limitations? I am not restricting the API, just expanding it. You can still use a controller, as I said and explained how an `assert` would work to validate the fields, hence as controller is still there.
I am labelling this as a proposal to add text/value parameter to textfield/textformfield
@maheshj01 can you please assign this to me? I can look into this

I don't get this change, there is `onChanged: onTextChanged` callback. If custom state handling is needed, it usually require large flexibility and onChanged is the way to go. I don't see the boilerplate.

@iamdipanshusingh Please wait until the secondary triage takes a look at this issue and this is prioritized. So that we know this is something flutter team would be interested to work on in the near future.

> I don't get this change, there is `onChanged: onTextChanged` callback. If custom state handling is needed, it usually require large flexibility and onChanged is the way to go. I don't see the boilerplate.

Most of the Flutter widgets work in a reactive manner where they inform they changed and they accept a value. By changing the value in the widget's constructor, the widget reacts.

TextField only does half of this, it only informs change. It can't be updated in a declarative manner, it can't react to change, unless we use the controller in an imperative manner, not declarative.

Thus, if my state manager (e.g. Bloc) has a new state, and I have to update a form with 10 TextFields, then I need to create 10 TextEditingControllers, their variables, initialise them, and not forget to dispose them, and set them when the state changes. That's a lot of extra boilerplate if you ever had to deal with forms and values coming from other sources.

The TextEditingController has its benefits, it allows us to control lots of things and also get notified of changes, but for the vast majority of cases we only want to set the text and get change notifications, and having this at the widget level will simplify these flows a lot.
Got it. Actually it would be useful in that specific case 👍

I've wanted to do this kind of thing for so long. This was the very first quirk that bugged me about Flutter when I started using it in 2018. Coming from React at the time, I much preferred the declarative approach. Since then I have been secretly hoping to find a good reason to make it declarative or at least find some clear counterexample to show that we shouldn't.

First hurdle: The parameter should probably be `textEditingValue` instead of `text` so that it supports selection and composing (see [TextEditingValue](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/flutter_test/TextEditingValue-class.html)). But unfortunately [onChanged](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/EditableText/onChanged.html) is called with a String instead of a TextEditingValue. Maybe we should also add an onChangedValue parameter or something.

Then we'd have to make sure that this approach generally works with all of our existing text input features. For example, [TextInputFormatter](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/TextInputFormatter-class.html) would be one to check. There's probably a lot of other stuff to worry about.

The first step should probably be to just try it out. Add textEditingValue and onChangedValue to EditableText and open a draft PR and see how it works.

If we can get it to work with everything and the API is not too confusing being alongside of TextEditingController, then I think I would approve that 👍

issues.title: Keyboard TextInputAction intermittently not changing when moving focus between fields
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Create two `TextField` widgets, the first with `TextInputAction.next`, the second with `TextInputAction.done`
2. Run the flutter app
3. Tap the first text field to focus it and bring up the android keyboard
4. Tap the "Next" button on the android keyboard to focus the second field

Tested on:
- Samsung S21 Ultra, Android 14
- Samsung Keyboard 5.8.20.7
- Flutter master 3.27.0-1.0.pre.660, and stable 3.24.4. Logs are from using Flutter master channel
- Affects both debug and release builds

### Expected results

The keyboard should display the "Done" keyboard action when the second field has been focussed after tapping the "Next" keyboard button on the first field. 

### Actual results

About 60% of the time, the keyboard continues to display the "Next" action on the second field after tapping the "Next" keyboard button on the first field. When tapping "Next" on the second field, the correct button "Done" displays briefly while the keyboard is being dismissed. 
I've included videos both of the issue occurring, and the roughly 40% of occasions when the issue does not occur and the code sample works as expected. 

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return const MaterialApp(
      title: 'Flutter Demo',
      home: MyHomePage(),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyHomePage({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: const Text('Input action not changing'),
      ),
      body: const Padding(
        padding: EdgeInsets.all(24),
        child: Column(
          mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.start,
          children: <Widget>[
            TextField(
              decoration: InputDecoration(
                hintText: 'Field with next action',
              ),
              textInputAction: TextInputAction.next,
            ),
            TextField(
              decoration: InputDecoration(
                hintText: 'Field with done action',
              ),
              textInputAction: TextInputAction.done,
            ),
          ],
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

```

comments:
# Triage Report

I was able to reproduce the issue using the provided code sample on the `stable (3.32.2)` and `master (3.33.0-1.0.pre.417)` channels.

I think this is duo to the cursor width of the __`SelectableText`__ widget.

See this discussion: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/95140

The difference between __`Text`__ and __`SelectableText`__ spacing can be reduced by setting __`cursorWidth`__ to zero:
```Dart
   SelectableText( cursorWidth: 0, ... )
```
But notice that the difference is not completely removed; there still remains 1 extra pixel in __`SelectableText`__, look:

```Dart
    Align( alignment: Alignment.topCenter, child: SizedBox(
       height: 150,
    // width: width,
       width: 73,
       child: Column( children: [
            Text( testString, style: textStyleGreen ),
            SelectableText( testString, style: textStyleRed ),
            SelectableText( cursorWidth: 0,  testString, style: textStyleRed ),
       ] )
    ) ),
```
 Using fixed `width = 73` | Using `layout`  width 
:-------------------------:|:-------------------------:
 ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e961b775-5f7f-481e-b5a1-9d3bb63a35e7) |![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6bcfb23d-4572-465b-9e9f-339827f80b52)
Any ways it is not clear, I checked the sources - default cursor width is 2 px, I tried to add 2 pixels in my project(not an example), and it is wired if I need to add 1 extra pixel...

@ifuterman would you be able to use SelectionArea instead of SelectableText? I imagine you won't have the same problem there. SelectableText uses EditableText under the hood, so there is a lot going on there that's not the same as the simple Text in your comparison.

I don't think that SelectableText is ignoring letterSpacing exactly, but there definitely is a difference in the layout between Text and SelectableText.
SelectionArea seems to be working fine.

issues.title: [BUG] Clipboard Changes Not Detected in Flutter on Linux (Wayland) Unless App is Focused
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Run a Flutter app on a Linux system using a `Wayland` session.
2. The app should listen for clipboard changes using a timer using inbuilt clipboard.
3. Copy new content to the clipboard while the Flutter app is running but not focused.
4. The clipboard change is not detected.
5. Switch focus back to the Flutter app window. The clipboard change is now detected and updated.
6. Run the same app on `X11`, and observe that clipboard changes are detected immediately without needing to switch window focus.

### Expected results

Clipboard changes should be detected in real-time on `Wayland`, just like in `X11`, without requiring the app to regain focus.

### Actual results

When a Flutter app that is designed to listen to clipboard in realtime and display it via a text widget is run in `Wayland` in Linux it doesn't listen to the changes in clipboard unless app window is focused, right after content has been copied. The same application would work perfectly in X11.

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
 void _startListening() async {
    Timer.periodic(const Duration(milliseconds: 100), (_) async {
      if (_pauseClipboard) return;
      ClipboardData? data = await Clipboard.getData(Clipboard.kTextPlain);

      final content = data?.text;
      if (content != null && content != _previousClipboardText) {
        _getClipboardText(content);
      }
    });
  }
```
</details>

comments: cc @cbracken

I was able to reproduce this. A couple notes:
1. The Sogou candidates window is positioned correctly on first text input -- i.e. before we start typing, we've notified it of the correct position within the window
2. The candidates windows doesn't move as you're typing, even though we are sending those events and the candidates window is repositioned with other IMEs (MS Pinyin input, MS Japanese, MS Korean, Google Japanese input), instead it remains at its initial position
3. If you start typing, but then cancel input (press ESC), then switch to the other text field and start typing, the candidates window _does_ update to the correct position.
4. Once you enter some composing text and hit enter to commit it, Sogou seems to lock itself into that position and the next time you start typing, the candidates window pops up at the position it was where you last committed text (which, because of item 2 above, is the initial position in a textfield) 
5. If you switch to Japanese input and input and commit some text, then switch to Sogou, the candidates window will appear at the position where the Japanese IME candidates window was last positioned

Flutter sends positioning events on focus and at each cursor movement. It seems like the Sogou IME is ignoring some subset of the positioning events we're sending, but listening to positioning events at (or near) a commit or result event. What's particularly surprising to me is item 3 above.
/cc @xyun52 who originally mentioned this bug in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/90503#issuecomment-942888715.

For reference in case anyone else gets to this before I do. The Sogou IME can be downloaded here: https://pinyin.sogou.com/

In my use case, I need to disable the inputAssistantItem (keyboard shortcuts bar) when using Apple Pencil input. Currently, it appears at the bottom of the screen, which causes an undesired layout shift in my app's UI.

I have explored common suggestions such as:

```dart
Scaffold(
  resizeToAvoidBottomInset: false,
)
```

Apple's official documentation:

issues.title: InteractiveViewer - text field is hidden by the keyboard when focused
issues.body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [X] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues)
- [X] I have read the [guide to filing a bug](https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports)

### Steps to reproduce

1. Run the code sample
2. Scroll all the way to the bottom.
3. Tap on the text field

### Expected results

The text field should move up with the keyboard so that it's not covered. 

### Actual results

The keyboard is hiding the text field.
If I swap this code
```dart
SizedBox(height: MediaQuery.of(context).size.height)
```
with this code
```dart
SizedBox(height: constraints.maxHeight)
```
Then it works as expected

### Code sample

<details><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

class MainScreen extends StatelessWidget {
  const MainScreen({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      body: SafeArea(
        child: LayoutBuilder(
          builder: (context, constraints) {
            return InteractiveViewer(
              constrained: false,
              child: SizedBox(
                width: constraints.maxWidth,
                child: Padding(
                  padding: const EdgeInsets.all(20),
                  child: Column(
                    children: [
                      const Text('Scroll to the bottom'),
                      SizedBox(height: MediaQuery.of(context).size.height),
                      const TextField(),
                    ],
                  ),
                ),
              ),
            );
          },
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

class MainApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MainApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      debugShowCheckedModeBanner: false,
      theme: ThemeData(
        inputDecorationTheme: const InputDecorationTheme(
          border: OutlineInputBorder(),
        ),
      ),
      home: const MainScreen(),
    );
  }
}

void main() {
  runApp(const MainApp());
}

```

issues.title: ☂️ Embedding keyboard states are not reset after hot restart
issues.body:
We should add a hook to `onPreEngineRestart` that frees and recreates the platform keyboard manager. Since the keyboard manager is stateful, failing to reset its state can cause state instability or even crash the app after a hot restart (Shift-R in CLI.) ## Progress - [x] Embedder: https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/28924 - [x] Linux - [x] Windows - [x] macOS - [ ] iOS - [ ] Android ## Reproduction ### Linux Reproduction, on an app with only a text field: 1. Start the app 2. Alt-Tab to the terminal 3. Hot restart 4. Switch back to the app. There are two unexpected behaviors: 1. The text field no longer displays texts. (This behavior will disappear after another hot restart.) 2. The CLI reports the crash log. (This behavior persists after another hot restart.)
